claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 4 years old and does not live with you.
  - Blake is 14 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 4 (under 16)
  - Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 14 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you find out if you're eligible for Child Benefit.

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you a few questions about your circumstances.

Let's start: **How many children are you claiming Child Benefit for, and what are their names and ages?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Hello! Yes, I'd like to find out if I'm eligible for Child Benefit. 

I have two children - Alex, who is 4 years old, and Blake, who is 14.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! It's great to know about Alex (4 years old) and Blake (14 years old).

Now, let me ask: **Do both Alex and Blake live with you?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Blake lives with me, but Alex doesn't - Alex lives with their other parent.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. Since Alex lives with their other parent, I have a follow-up question about that situation.

**Is the other parent (the one Alex lives with) already claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, they're not claiming Child Benefit for Alex. I do contribute to Alex's upkeep though - I help out with things like clothes and school expenses.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that detail. Since you contribute to Alex's upkeep but Alex doesn't live with you, I need to understand a bit more about your arrangement.

**Do you contribute at least the same amount towards Alex's upkeep as anyone else does (including the parent Alex lives with)?** 

In other words, is your contribution equal to or more than what the other parent spends on Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, I contribute regularly towards Alex's costs - I give money each week to help with their upkeep. I'd say my contribution is at least equal to what the other parent provides, yes.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. That's helpful to know about your contribution to Alex's care.

Now I have a couple more questions to complete the assessment.

**Are you (the person who would be claiming Child Benefit) responsible for both children?** By "responsible," I mean are you someone who either has the child living with you, or contributes to the cost of looking after them at a rate that's at least equal to the Child Benefit amount?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I'm responsible for both of them. Blake lives with me, and as I mentioned, I contribute regularly to Alex's upkeep.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. Just a couple more questions to complete the assessment.

**Are both you and the children living in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, we all live in the UK. I'm in Birmingham.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great, thank you! Now for my final questions:

**Regarding Blake (who is 14), is Blake in full-time education or registered with a careers service/training provider?**

And also, **is there anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for either Alex or Blake?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Blake is in school - he's in Year 9, so yes, full-time education.

And no, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for either of them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Let me just check the eligibility rules to make sure I give you accurate information.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for your patience. I now have all the information I need to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for both Alex and Blake.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Based on the information you've provided, here's my assessment of your Child Benefit eligibility:

---

## Overall Result: You appear to be **eligible** for Child Benefit for both children 🎉

---

### **Blake (14 years old)** ✅ Eligible

Your eligibility for Blake is straightforward because:
- Blake is under 16 years old
- Blake lives with you, so you're clearly responsible for them
- Blake is in full-time education (Year 9)
- You live in the UK
- No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Blake

---

### **Alex (4 years old)** ✅ Eligible

Although Alex lives with their other parent, you can still claim Child Benefit because:
- Alex is under 16 years old
- You live in the UK
- You contribute regularly to Alex's upkeep at a level at least equal to the Child Benefit amount
- No one else is currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex

---

### Important Points to Note:

1. **Only one person can claim Child Benefit per child.** If Alex's other parent decides to claim in the future, you would need to agree between yourselves who should receive it. If you cannot agree, HMRC will decide.

2. **High Income Tax Charge:** If you or your partner have an individual income over £60,000, you may need to pay a tax charge on the Child Benefit you receive.

Would you like any further information about applying for Child Benefit?

reasonings: Alex (4 years old): Although Alex does not live with you, you may still be eligible for Child Benefit because: (1) Alex is under 16 years old; (2) You live in the UK; (3) You contribute regularly to Alex's upkeep, and you've stated your contribution is at least equal to what the other parent provides; (4) No one else is currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex. According to the rules, you can continue to receive Child Benefit for a child who lives with someone else if you contribute to your child's upkeep by at least the same amount as the Child Benefit payment. Since you are making regular contributions and no one else is claiming, you would be eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake (14 years old): You are eligible for Child Benefit for Blake because: (1) Blake is under 16 years old; (2) Blake lives with you, making you clearly responsible for them; (3) Blake is in full-time education (Year 9); (4) You live in the UK; (5) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Blake. All the standard eligibility criteria are met.
arguments.overall_reasoning: Based on the information provided, you appear to be eligible for Child Benefit for both children. For Blake, eligibility is straightforward as Blake lives with you and is in full-time education. For Alex, although Alex lives with the other parent, you can still claim Child Benefit because you contribute regularly to Alex's upkeep at a level at least equal to the Child Benefit amount, and no one else is currently claiming for Alex. You should be aware that only one person can claim Child Benefit per child, so if the other parent decides to claim for Alex in the future, the Child Benefit Office would need to determine who should receive the payment.

Since you are making regular contributions and no one else is claiming, you would be eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.

All the standard eligibility criteria are met.

You should be aware that only one person can claim Child Benefit per child, so if the other parent decides to claim for Alex in the future, the Child Benefit Office would need to determine who should receive the payment.
